question: [File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Reentrancy or callback-driven state corruption across take, repay, withdraw, liquidation, fee claim, or collateral flows'] [Function: repay()] Can an unprivileged borrower acting as the repay callback receiver under preconditions where marketState[id].withdrawable > 0 and position[id][onBehalf].debt >= units trigger the sequence repay(units, onBehalf, callback=self) -> onRepay() reenters withdraw(units, lender, receiver) before safeTransferFrom pulls the loan token, violating the invariant that contract.balanceOf(loanToken) >= marketState[id].withdrawable at all times, causing scoped impact: lender drains existing withdrawable loan tokens before the repay payer has transferred funds, leaving the contract insolvent? Proof idea: fuzz units in [1, withdrawable], assert balanceOf(loanToken) >= withdrawable after every internal state change including mid-callback.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Reentrancy or callback-driven state corruption across take, repay, withdraw, liquidation, fee claim, or collateral flows'] [Function: liquidate()] Can an unprivileged liquidator acting as the liquidate callback receiver under preconditions where repaidUnits > 0 and marketState[id].withdrawable is increased before onLiquidate is called trigger the sequence liquidate(seizedAssets=0, repaidUnits=X, callback=self) -> collateral transferred to receiver -> withdrawable += X -> onLiquidate() reenters withdraw(X, lender, receiver) before safeTransferFrom pulls the loan token, violating the invariant that contract.balanceOf(loanToken) >= marketState[id].withdrawable, causing scoped impact: lender withdraws X loan tokens that have not yet been transferred in by the liquidator, draining other lenders' withdrawable assets? Proof idea: stateful fuzz repaidUnits and existing withdrawable, assert balanceOf >= withdrawable holds after every sub-step.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Reentrancy or callback-driven state corruption across take, repay, withdraw, liquidation, fee claim, or collateral flows'] [Function: take()] Can an unprivileged taker acting as the buyer callback (takerCallback) under preconditions where marketState[id].withdrawable > 0 and the buyer's credit was just increased by buyerCreditIncrease trigger the sequence take(offer.buy=false, takerCallback=self) -> position state updated -> onBuy() reenters withdraw(min(buyerCreditIncrease, withdrawable), buyer, receiver) before safeTransferFrom pulls buyerAssets from payer, violating the invariant that contract.balanceOf(loanToken) >= marketState[id].withdrawable, causing scoped impact: buyer drains pre-existing withdrawable loan tokens using freshly minted credit before the payer has transferred any tokens? Proof idea: unit test with existing withdrawable=W, buyerCreditIncrease=W, assert balanceOf >= withdrawable after onBuy reentry.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Reentrancy or callback-driven state corruption across take, repay, withdraw, liquidation, fee claim, or collateral flows'] [Function: repay()] Can an unprivileged callback receiver under preconditions where position[id][onBehalf].debt = D and the callback is authorized for onBehalf trigger the sequence repay(D, onBehalf, callback=self) -> debt reduced to 0 -> withdrawable += D -> onRepay() reenters repay(D, onBehalf, callback=address(0)) attempting to reduce debt below zero, violating the invariant that position[id][onBehalf].debt must not underflow, causing scoped impact: arithmetic underflow reverts the outer repay, or if debt is re-incremented by a concurrent take, double-reduces debt creating phantom withdrawable? Proof idea: fuzz debt amounts and reentry depth, assert position.debt never underflows and withdrawable never exceeds sum of all repaid units.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Reentrancy or callback-driven state corruption across take, repay, withdraw, liquidation, fee claim, or collateral flows'] [Function: liquidate()] Can an unprivileged liquidator acting as the liquidate callback under preconditions where badDebt > 0 and lossFactor is updated before onLiquidate is called trigger the sequence liquidate(borrower, callback=self) -> lossFactor updated -> onLiquidate() reenters updatePosition(market, lender) to crystallize the new lossFactor and slash lender credit -> outer liquidate continues with stale totalUnits, violating the invariant that continuousFeeCredit is correctly scaled relative to lossFactor after bad debt realization, causing scoped impact: continuousFeeCredit accounting desync from actual lender credit, enabling overclaim or underclaim of continuous fees? Proof idea: invariant test asserting continuousFeeCredit / totalUnits ratio is preserved across liquidate+updatePosition reentry.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Reentrancy or callback-driven state corruption across take, repay, withdraw, liquidation, fee claim, or collateral flows'] [Function: take() / LIQUIDATION_LOCK_SLOT] Can an unprivileged taker under preconditions where the seller has debt and the taker controls the seller callback (offer.callback) trigger the sequence take(offer.buy=true, offer.callback=self) -> LIQUIDATION_LOCK_SLOT set for seller via tExchange -> onSell() reenters take(offer.buy=true, offer.callback=self) for the same seller -> inner take sets wasLocked=true -> inner take's onSell reenters liquidate(seller) -> liquidationLocked(id, seller) returns true -> NotLiquidatable() reverts, violating the invariant that LIQUIDATION_LOCK_SLOT correctly prevents liquidation of the seller during all nested callback depths, causing scoped impact: confirm lock holds across arbitrary reentry depth or identify depth at which it fails? Proof idea: fuzz reentry depth 1..10, assert liquidate(seller) always reverts with NotLiquidatable during any onSell callback.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Reentrancy or callback-driven state corruption across take, repay, withdraw, liquidation, fee claim, or collateral flows'] [Function: take() / LIQUIDATION_LOCK_SLOT] Can an unprivileged attacker under preconditions where seller S has debt and a take is in progress (LIQUIDATION_LOCK_SLOT set for S) trigger the sequence take(seller=S) -> onBuy() reenters flashLoan([loanToken], [amount], callback=self) -> onFlashLoan() calls liquidate(borrower=S) -> liquidationLocked(id, S) is checked, violating the invariant that LIQUIDATION_LOCK_SLOT keyed by (id, seller) prevents liquidation of S from any reentry path including flashLoan callbacks, causing scoped impact: if flashLoan callback bypasses the lock, seller S can be liquidated mid-take while their position is partially updated? Proof idea: unit test asserting liquidate(S) reverts inside onFlashLoan when S is locked by an outer take.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Reentrancy or callback-driven state corruption across take, repay, withdraw, liquidation, fee claim, or collateral flows'] [Function: take() / LIQUIDATION_LOCK_SLOT] Can an unprivileged attacker under preconditions where wasLocked=false for the outer take and the seller callback reenters take() with the same seller trigger the sequence outer take -> wasLocked=false -> onSell() reenters take(same seller) -> inner wasLocked=true -> inner take completes without releasing lock -> outer take completes and releases lock (wasLocked=false), violating the invariant that the lock is released exactly once per outermost take and not prematurely, causing scoped impact: confirm or deny that the wasLocked pattern in tExchange correctly handles nested takes without leaving the seller permanently locked or prematurely unlocked within the transaction? Proof idea: differential test comparing liquidationLocked(id, seller) before and after nested take sequences.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Reentrancy or callback-driven state corruption across take, repay, withdraw, liquidation, fee claim, or collateral flows'] [Function: liquidate()] Can an unprivileged liquidator acting as the liquidate callback under preconditions where the borrower still has debt after bad debt realization and seizedAssets > 0 trigger the sequence liquidate(borrower, seizedAssets=X, callback=self) -> collateral transferred -> withdrawable += repaidUnits -> debt reduced -> onLiquidate() reenters liquidate(borrower, seizedAssets=Y) for the same borrower -> second liquidation checks NotLiquidatable() on already-reduced debt, violating the invariant that a borrower cannot be liquidated twice in the same transaction for more than their total debt, causing scoped impact: double-liquidation seizes more collateral than the borrower's debt justifies, creating bad debt or over-seizing? Proof idea: fuzz seizedAssets X and Y, assert sum of repaidUnits across both liquidations <= original debt.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Reentrancy or callback-driven state corruption across take, repay, withdraw, liquidation, fee claim, or collateral flows'] [Function: liquidate() / TIME_TO_MAX_LIF] Can an unprivileged liquidator acting as the liquidate callback under preconditions where block.timestamp is between maturity and maturity + TIME_TO_MAX_LIF (15 minutes) trigger the sequence liquidate(postMaturityMode=true, seizedAssets=X, callback=self) -> lif computed as WAD + (maxLif-WAD)*(block.timestamp-maturity)/TIME_TO_MAX_LIF -> onLiquidate() reenters liquidate(postMaturityMode=true, seizedAssets=Y) at the same block.timestamp -> second liquidation uses the same lif, violating the invariant that total seized collateral across reentrant liquidations is bounded by the borrower's debt times lif/ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE, causing scoped impact: attacker seizes more collateral than the borrower's remaining debt justifies at the current LIF? Proof idea: fuzz timestamp in [maturity, maturity+900], assert sum(seizedAssets) * price / ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE / lif <= original debt.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Reentrancy or callback-driven state corruption across take, repay, withdraw, liquidation, fee claim, or collateral flows'] [Function: take() / onBuy] Can an unprivileged taker acting as the buyer callback under preconditions where the buyer has no prior credit and buyerCreditIncrease > 0 trigger the sequence take(offer.buy=false, takerCallback=self) -> buyerPos.credit += buyerCreditIncrease -> onBuy() reenters take(offer.buy=false, takerCallback=self) with a second offer for the same buyer -> second take calls _updatePosition(buyer) which reads the updated credit from the first take -> second take's buyerCreditIncrease is computed against already-updated state, violating the invariant that each take's credit/debt accounting is independent and non-composable within a single callback chain, causing scoped impact: buyer accumulates more credit than the sum of individual takes would produce due to stale _updatePosition interactions? Proof idea: invariant test asserting buyer.credit after N reentrant takes equals sum of individual buyerCreditIncrease values.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Reentrancy or callback-driven state corruption across take, repay, withdraw, liquidation, fee claim, or collateral flows'] [Function: take() / onSell] Can an unprivileged maker acting as the seller callback (offer.callback) under preconditions where the seller has credit > 0 and sellerCreditDecrease > 0 trigger the sequence take(offer.buy=true, offer.callback=self) -> sellerPos.credit -= sellerCreditDecrease -> onSell() reenters withdrawCollateral(seller, collateralIndex, assets) before the health check at line 476, violating the invariant that the seller's health is checked after all state changes including any collateral withdrawal during the callback, causing scoped impact: seller withdraws collateral during onSell making their position unhealthy, but the outer take's health check at line 476 catches this and reverts - confirm or deny that the health check at line 476 is sufficient to prevent this? Proof idea: unit test withdrawing max collateral in onSell, assert SellerIsLiquidatable() is thrown.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Reentrancy or callback-driven state corruption across take, repay, withdraw, liquidation, fee claim, or collateral flows'] [Function: take() / onSell] Can an unprivileged maker acting as the seller callback under preconditions where the seller has zero debt (sellerDebtIncrease > 0 from this take) trigger the sequence take(offer.buy=true, offer.callback=self) -> sellerPos.debt += sellerDebtIncrease -> onSell() reenters supplyCollateral(seller, index, assets) to add collateral -> then reenters withdrawCollateral(seller, index, assets) to remove the same collateral -> outer health check at line 476 sees seller with debt but no collateral, violating the invariant that the health check at line 476 reflects the final collateral state after all callback-driven mutations, causing scoped impact: seller borrows without net collateral if the health check is bypassed by the add-then-remove pattern? Proof idea: unit test supply+withdraw in onSell, assert SellerIsLiquidatable() reverts the outer take.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Reentrancy or callback-driven state corruption across take, repay, withdraw, liquidation, fee claim, or collateral flows'] [Function: take() / claimableSettlementFee] Can an unprivileged taker acting as the buyer callback under preconditions where the taker is also the feeClaimer and claimableSettlementFee[loanToken] is increased before onBuy is called trigger the sequence take(takerCallback=self) -> claimableSettlementFee[loanToken] += (buyerAssets - sellerAssets) -> onBuy() calls claimSettlementFee(loanToken, amount, receiver) -> claimableSettlementFee reduced -> safeTransfer sends fee tokens -> outer take then calls safeTransferFrom(payer, this, buyerAssets-sellerAssets), violating the invariant that claimableSettlementFee is only claimable after the corresponding loan tokens have been transferred into the contract, causing scoped impact: feeClaimer drains settlement fee tokens from the contract's existing balance before the payer has transferred the fee portion? Proof idea: unit test with feeClaimer=takerCallback, assert contract balance never goes negative during take.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Reentrancy or callback-driven state corruption across take, repay, withdraw, liquidation, fee claim, or collateral flows'] [Function: claimContinuousFee() / ERC20 hook] Can an unprivileged attacker controlling a loan token with ERC777-like transfer hooks under preconditions where continuousFeeCredit > 0 and the feeClaimer is the attacker trigger the sequence claimContinuousFee(market, amount, receiver) -> continuousFeeCredit -= amount, totalUnits -= amount, withdrawable -= amount -> safeTransfer(loanToken, receiver, amount) -> ERC777 tokensReceived hook reenters claimContinuousFee(market, amount, receiver) -> second call reduces continuousFeeCredit, totalUnits, withdrawable again, violating the invariant that continuousFeeCredit, totalUnits, and withdrawable are each reduced exactly once per claim, causing scoped impact: double-claim drains withdrawable below what lenders can legitimately withdraw, creating insolvency? Proof idea: unit test with ERC777 hook reentering claimContinuousFee, assert withdrawable >= sum of all lender credits after both calls.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Reentrancy or callback-driven state corruption across take, repay, withdraw, liquidation, fee claim, or collateral flows'] [Function: claimSettlementFee() / ERC20 hook] Can an unprivileged attacker controlling a loan token with ERC777-like transfer hooks under preconditions where claimableSettlementFee[token] > 0 and the feeClaimer is the attacker trigger the sequence claimSettlementFee(token, amount, receiver) -> claimableSettlementFee[token] -= amount -> safeTransfer(token, receiver, amount) -> ERC777 tokensReceived hook reenters claimSettlementFee(token, amount, receiver) -> second call reduces claimableSettlementFee by amount again (underflow or double drain), violating the invariant that claimableSettlementFee[token] accurately tracks unclaimed settlement fees and contract balance covers it, causing scoped impact: double-claim of settlement fees drains loan tokens that belong to lenders or borrowers? Proof idea: unit test with ERC777 hook reentering claimSettlementFee, assert claimableSettlementFee[token] == 0 and no extra tokens transferred.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Reentrancy or callback-driven state corruption across take, repay, withdraw, liquidation, fee claim, or collateral flows'] [Function: withdrawCollateral() / ERC20 hook] Can an unprivileged borrower controlling a collateral token with ERC777-like transfer hooks under preconditions where position[id][borrower].debt > 0 and the health check passes before safeTransfer trigger the sequence withdrawCollateral(market, index, assets, borrower, receiver) -> position.collateral[index] -= assets -> isHealthy() passes -> safeTransfer(collateralToken, receiver, assets) -> ERC777 tokensReceived hook reenters withdrawCollateral(market, index, remainingCollateral, borrower, receiver) -> second health check uses already-reduced collateral, violating the invariant that a borrower cannot withdraw collateral that would make them unhealthy, causing scoped impact: borrower withdraws all collateral in two steps leaving debt uncollateralized? Proof idea: unit test with ERC777 hook reentering withdrawCollateral, assert isHealthy(borrower) after both withdrawals.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Reentrancy or callback-driven state corruption across take, repay, withdraw, liquidation, fee claim, or collateral flows'] [Function: take() / multicall] Can an unprivileged taker under preconditions where a sell offer exists and the market has withdrawable > 0 trigger the sequence multicall([take(offer.buy=false, units=U), withdraw(U, taker, receiver)]) where the take increases the seller's debt and the withdraw drains existing withdrawable, violating the invariant that a taker who just borrowed cannot immediately withdraw loan tokens that belong to other lenders, causing scoped impact: taker borrows and immediately withdraws pre-existing withdrawable assets in the same transaction, effectively stealing from lenders? Proof idea: unit test multicall sequence, assert taker's net loan token gain equals zero (they borrowed but didn't receive loan tokens from the take).

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Reentrancy or callback-driven state corruption across take, repay, withdraw, liquidation, fee claim, or collateral flows'] [Function: repay() / multicall] Can an unprivileged borrower under preconditions where position[id][borrower].debt = D and marketState[id].withdrawable = W trigger the sequence multicall([repay(D, borrower, callback=address(0)), withdraw(D, lender, receiver)]) where repay increases withdrawable to W+D and withdraw drains W+D, violating the invariant that withdraw can only drain tokens that have been transferred into the contract, causing scoped impact: if the contract's balance was only W before the repay, the withdraw of W+D would fail - but if balance >= W+D, lender front-runs other lenders by withdrawing in the same multicall as the repay? Proof idea: fuzz W and D, assert contract balance >= 0 after multicall and no lender can withdraw more than their credit.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Reentrancy or callback-driven state corruption across take, repay, withdraw, liquidation, fee claim, or collateral flows'] [Function: flashLoan() / onFlashLoan] Can an unprivileged attacker under preconditions where a borrower B has debt and is unhealthy trigger the sequence flashLoan([loanToken], [repaidUnits], callback=self) -> onFlashLoan() calls repay(repaidUnits, B, address(0)) -> withdrawable += repaidUnits -> then calls withdraw(repaidUnits, lender, attacker) -> then returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS -> safeTransferFrom pulls repaidUnits back, violating the invariant that flashLoan callbacks cannot net-drain the contract's loan token balance by exploiting the repay+withdraw sequence within the callback, causing scoped impact: attacker uses flash loan to repay B's debt, withdraw the repaid tokens to themselves, and return the flash loan from their own funds, effectively stealing from lenders? Proof idea: unit test flashLoan+repay+withdraw sequence, assert attacker's net token gain is zero.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Reentrancy or callback-driven state corruption across take, repay, withdraw, liquidation, fee claim, or collateral flows'] [Function: take() / onBuy / MAX_COLLATERALS_PER_BORROWER] Can an unprivileged taker acting as the buyer callback under preconditions where the buyer already has MAX_COLLATERALS_PER_BORROWER - 1 = 15 activated collaterals trigger the sequence take(takerCallback=self) -> buyerPos.credit += buyerCreditIncrease -> onBuy() calls supplyCollateral(market, newIndex, 1, buyer) -> collateralBitmap.setBit(newIndex) -> countBits(newCollateralBitmap) == 16 <= MAX_COLLATERALS_PER_BORROWER -> succeeds -> outer take completes, violating the invariant that MAX_COLLATERALS_PER_BORROWER is enforced atomically and cannot be exceeded by interleaving supplyCollateral within a take callback, causing scoped impact: buyer reaches exactly 16 collaterals during onBuy, then a subsequent supplyCollateral in the same tx would revert - confirm the limit is correctly enforced at 16? Proof idea: unit test adding 16th collateral in onBuy, assert TooManyActivatedCollaterals on 17th.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Reentrancy or callback-driven state corruption across take, repay, withdraw, liquidation, fee claim, or collateral flows'] [Function: liquidate() / onLiquidate / MAX_COLLATERALS_PER_BORROWER] Can an unprivileged liquidator acting as the liquidate callback under preconditions where the borrower has MAX_COLLATERALS_PER_BORROWER collaterals and the liquidation clears one collateral (newCollateral==0) trigger the sequence liquidate(borrower, collateralIndex=i, seizedAssets=all, callback=self) -> collateralBitmap.clearBit(i) -> onLiquidate() calls supplyCollateral(market, i, 1, borrower) -> collateralBitmap.setBit(i) -> borrower now has MAX_COLLATERALS_PER_BORROWER collaterals again -> outer liquidate continues with stale collateralBitmap snapshot, violating the invariant that the collateralBitmap used in the liquidation loop reflects the final state after all callback mutations, causing scoped impact: borrower's collateral is re-added during onLiquidate, making subsequent health checks use an incorrect bitmap? Proof idea: unit test re-supplying cleared collateral in onLiquidate, assert borrower's bitmap and health are consistent after liquidation.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Reentrancy or callback-driven state corruption across take, repay, withdraw, liquidation, fee claim, or collateral flows'] [Function: take() / onBuy / setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged taker acting as the buyer callback under preconditions where the buyer has credit trigger the sequence take(takerCallback=self) -> buyerPos.credit += buyerCreditIncrease -> onBuy() calls setIsAuthorized(attacker, true, buyer) (if authorized for buyer) -> attacker is now authorized for buyer -> outer take completes -> attacker calls withdraw(credit, buyer, attacker) in a subsequent transaction, violating the invariant that authorization changes during callbacks cannot be used to immediately drain the position within the same callback chain, causing scoped impact: attacker gains authorization over buyer's position during onBuy and drains credit in a follow-up call? Proof idea: unit test setIsAuthorized in onBuy, assert attacker cannot withdraw buyer's credit in the same transaction.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Reentrancy or callback-driven state corruption across take, repay, withdraw, liquidation, fee claim, or collateral flows'] [Function: take() / onBuy / setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged taker acting as the buyer callback under preconditions where the taker is authorized for the buyer trigger the sequence take(takerCallback=self) -> onBuy() calls setIsAuthorized(attacker, true, buyer) -> then reenters withdraw(buyerCreditIncrease, buyer, attacker) using the new authorization -> withdraw reduces credit and withdrawable -> outer take's safeTransferFrom pulls buyerAssets from payer, violating the invariant that credit created in a take cannot be immediately withdrawn within the same take's callback before the payer has transferred tokens, causing scoped impact: attacker drains buyer's newly created credit before the payer pays, leaving the contract short by buyerAssets? Proof idea: unit test setIsAuthorized+withdraw in onBuy, assert contract balance >= withdrawable after callback.

[File: 'src/libraries/ConstantsLib.sol -> Scope: Critical Reentrancy or callback-driven state corruption across take, repay, withdraw, liquidation, fee claim, or collateral flows'] [Function: take() / consumed / setConsumed] Can an unprivileged maker under preconditions where consumed[maker][group] = C and offer.maxAssets = C (fully consumed) trigger the sequence take(offer, units=0) -> consumed[maker][group] += 0 -> newConsumed = C <= maxAssets -> onBuy() or onSell() callback executes (per comment at line 93: 'Midnight can call the callback of offers through a no-op take, even if those offers have consumed==max') -> callback calls setConsumed(group, type(uint256).max, maker) to cancel the group -> then reenters take(offer, units=U) -> consumed check fails with ConsumedUnits/ConsumedAssets, violating the invariant that a no-op take's callback cannot be used to reset or cancel the consumption group mid-execution, causing scoped impact: maker uses no-op take callback to cancel their own group and prevent legitimate fills? Proof idea: unit test no-op take + setConsumed in callback, assert subsequent take with units>0 reverts.
